title: Dubu Jorim Spicy Braised Tofu
description: Dubu Jorim is a beloved Korean comfort food where firm tofu is pan-fried until golden, then gently braised in a vibrant, savory, and spicy gochugaru-based sauce. The tofu absorbs the rich flavors beautifully, offering a tender interior with a slight chew, making it a perfectly satisfying and wholesome vegetarian dish enjoyed with a bowl of warm rice.

ingredients.notes: 1 thinly sliced for garnish, 1 cut into 2-inch pieces for braising
optional, for deeper color and flavor

instructions.title: Prepare and Press the Tofu
instructions.text: Remove tofu from its packaging and drain excess water. Place the tofu blocks on a plate lined with paper towels, cover with more paper towels, and place a heavy object (like a cutting board with cans) on top to press for at least 20-30 minutes. This removes excess moisture, ensuring the tofu fries better and absorbs flavors more effectively. Once pressed, slice each block of tofu into 8-10 rectangular pieces, about 1/2 inch thick.

instructions.title: Pan-Fry the Tofu
instructions.text: Heat 3-4 tablespoons of vegetable oil in a large non-stick skillet over medium-high heat. Carefully place the sliced tofu pieces in a single layer, ensuring not to overcrowd the pan. Fry for 3-5 minutes per side until each side is golden brown and slightly crisp. This step adds texture and prevents the tofu from breaking apart during braising. Remove the fried tofu from the skillet and set aside on a plate lined with paper towels to drain any excess oil.

instructions.title: Prepare the Braising Sauce (Yangnyeomjang)
instructions.text: While the tofu is frying, prepare the braising sauce. In a medium bowl, combine the gochugaru, ganjang (soy sauce), minced garlic, sesame oil, sugar (or rice syrup), gochujang (if using), and black pepper. Whisk all ingredients together until well combined. This is your flavorful Korean 'yangnyeomjang'.

instructions.title: Assemble for Braising
instructions.text: In a wide, shallow pot or large skillet (preferably one with a lid), arrange the pan-fried tofu pieces in a single layer. Scatter the 2-inch pieces of green onion over and around the tofu. Pour the prepared braising sauce evenly over the tofu. Then, pour 1.5 cups of water or vegetable broth into the pot.

instructions.title: Braise the Tofu
instructions.text: Bring the liquid to a gentle boil over medium-high heat. Once boiling, reduce the heat to medium-low, cover the pot, and let it simmer for 15-20 minutes. Occasionally, gently spoon the sauce over the tofu to ensure even flavor distribution. Continue to simmer, uncovered for the last 5-10 minutes, or until the sauce has reduced and thickened to your desired consistency, coating the tofu beautifully.

instructions.title: Garnish and Serve
instructions.text: Once the Dubu Jorim is ready, remove it from the heat. Garnish generously with the thinly sliced fresh green onions and a sprinkle of white sesame seeds. Serve immediately with a bowl of warm, freshly cooked Korean short-grain rice and other banchan (side dishes) for a complete and authentic Korean meal. Enjoy the comforting, spicy, and savory flavors!

tips: Always press your tofu thoroughly before frying to achieve a firmer texture and allow it to absorb the braising sauce more effectively.
For an extra layer of umami, consider using a good quality, naturally brewed Korean soy sauce (Ganjang) and fresh garlic.
Dubu Jorim is best served immediately with hot rice, but leftovers can be stored in the refrigerator for up to 3 days and gently reheated.
article:
## History of Dubu Jorim Spicy Braised Tofu

Tofu (dubu) has been a staple in Korean cuisine for centuries, introduced from China and quickly adopted for its nutritional value and versatility. It became a significant source of protein, especially in a historically agrarian society, and was prized for its ability to absorb flavors. Jorim, a traditional Korean braising technique, involves simmering ingredients in a savory, often spicy, sauce until it reduces and coats the food. Dubu Jorim specifically emerged as a popular and accessible dish, offering a delicious and affordable way to enjoy tofu. Its simple yet profound flavors made it a beloved banchan (side dish) that transcended social classes, appearing on tables from humble homes to more elaborate feasts. It embodies the Korean philosophy of utilizing wholesome, basic ingredients to create deeply satisfying and comforting meals, providing both nourishment and warmth.

## Why This Recipe Works

This Dubu Jorim recipe shines due to its masterful balance of flavors and textures. Pressing and pan-frying the tofu creates a firm, slightly chewy exterior that perfectly contrasts with its tender, porous interior. This pre-treatment also allows the tofu to better absorb the complex braising sauce. The sauce itself is a symphony of Korean staples: the fiery depth of gochugaru, the umami richness of ganjang, the aromatic punch of garlic, and a hint of sweetness from sugar or rice syrup, all rounded out by nutty sesame oil. As the tofu simmers, it drinks in these layered flavors, transforming into a deeply savory, spicy, and comforting dish. The reduction of the sauce concentrates its essence, ensuring every bite is packed with authentic Korean taste.

## Chef Tips for Perfection

For the perfect Dubu Jorim, proper tofu preparation is key. Always use extra-firm tofu and press it thoroughly for at least 20-30 minutes to remove excess water; this prevents crumbling and allows for better browning and flavor absorption. When pan-frying, don't overcrowd the pan and ensure each side is beautifully golden brown for that desirable textural contrast. Adjust the gochugaru to your spice preference, but don't skip it entirely, as it's essential for the dish's authentic character. For a richer, more vibrant red hue, a touch of gochujang can be added to the sauce. Lastly, braise gently over medium-low heat, allowing the sauce to slowly reduce and thicken, ensuring the tofu is thoroughly infused without becoming mushy.

## Serving Suggestions

Dubu Jorim is a quintessential Korean banchan, meaning it's traditionally served as a side dish alongside a bowl of freshly cooked short-grain white rice. The simple rice acts as a perfect canvas for the bold, spicy, and savory flavors of the tofu. To complete a Korean meal, pair it with other complementary banchan such as kimchi (fermented cabbage), various namul (seasoned vegetable sides like spinach or bean sprouts), or a light soup like Doenjang Jjigae (soybean paste stew). The robust flavors of Dubu Jorim also make it a satisfying main course for a vegetarian meal, especially when served with a refreshing vegetable salad or a simple clear broth to balance the richness.

jsonLd.keywords: Korean, Korean, authentic, traditional, vegetarian, Korean, Korean, authentic, traditional, vegetarian
recipeIngredient: 2 stalks Green onions (1 thinly sliced for garnish, 1 cut into 2-inch pieces for braising)
2-3 tablespoons Gochugaru (Korean chili flakes) (adjust to spice preference)
1 tablespoon Gochujang (Korean chili paste) (optional, for deeper color and flavor)
